Cabernet Sauvignon.desc: The noble black grape of the Médoc — thick-skinned, late-ripening, giving deeply structured, tannic, age-worthy reds.

Merlot.desc: Bordeaux's plush, early-ripening partner to Cabernet; dominant on the Right Bank around Pomerol and Saint-Émilion.
Merlot.profile: Plum, black cherry, soft and round

Cabernet Franc.desc: Aromatic, lighter-bodied Bordeaux variety that shines alone in the Loire (Chinon, Bourgueil).

Petit Verdot.desc: A late-ripening Bordeaux blending grape adding colour, structure and spice in small doses.

Malbec.desc: The 'black wine' grape of Cahors in the South-West, deeply coloured and rustic at home.

Tannat.desc: Madiran's ferociously tannic black grape, softened by oak and time.

Négrette.desc: The signature grape of Fronton near Toulouse, giving perfumed, soft reds.

Fer Servadou.desc: A firm, peppery South-West variety (Marcillac, Gaillac), also called Braucol.

Braucol.desc: Gaillac's name for Fer Servadou — rustic, spicy, herb-edged reds.

Duras.desc: A peppery, perfumed black grape native to Gaillac in the South-West.

Prunelart.desc: An old, nearly-lost South-West variety revived around Gaillac; dark and sloe-like.

Pinot Noir.desc: Burgundy's heartbreak grape — thin-skinned and terroir-transparent, the red of the Côte d'Or and a base of Champagne.
Pinot Noir.profile: Red cherry, rose, forest floor, silky

Gamay.desc: Beaujolais' juicy, granite-loving black grape, made by carbonic maceration for gulpable freshness.

Syrah.desc: The Northern Rhône's dark, peppery grape behind Hermitage and Côte-Rôtie.

Grenache.desc: Warm-climate workhorse of the Southern Rhône and Châteauneuf-du-Pape; high alcohol, sweet red fruit.

Mourvèdre.desc: Sun-loving Southern grape (Bandol, Châteauneuf) giving meaty, structured, ageworthy reds.

Cinsault.desc: A soft, fragrant Southern blending grape, key to pale Provençal rosé.

Carignan.desc: High-yielding Languedoc grape, profound from old bushvines on schist.

Nielluccio.desc: Corsica's main red (a relative of Sangiovese), structured and savoury in Patrimonio.

Sciaccarello.desc: A peppery, perfumed Corsican red grape grown around Ajaccio.

Carcaghjolu Neru.desc: A rare indigenous Corsican black variety being revived by island growers.

Barbarossa.desc: An old Corsican grape (no relation to the Italian one) used in island reds and rosés.

Folle Noire.desc: The black grape of Bellet above Nice, giving fragrant, structured reds.

Braquet.desc: An aromatic Bellet (Nice) grape used for perfumed reds and rosés.

Poulsard.desc: Jura's pale, delicate red — barely tinted but wonderfully perfumed.

Trousseau.desc: The Jura's deeper, firmer red grape, best on warm gravel sites.

Mondeuse.desc: Savoie's characterful black grape giving peppery, dark, alpine reds.

Aleatico.desc: An aromatic Muscat-scented red grown in Corsica, often sweet.

Pinot Meunier.desc: The fruity, frost-hardy third grape of Champagne, adding roundness to blends.

Chardonnay.desc: Burgundy's great white, from steely Chablis to rich Meursault, and a pillar of Champagne (Blanc de Blancs).

Sauvignon Blanc.desc: The Loire's zesty white star (Sancerre, Pouilly-Fumé), all nerve and aromatics.

Chenin Blanc.desc: The Loire's chameleon (Vouvray, Savennières) — dry to lusciously sweet, always with cutting acidity.

Riesling.desc: Alsace's noblest white — precise, mineral and long-lived, dry to nobly sweet.

Gewurztraminer.desc: Alsace's exotic, pink-skinned aromatic white, opulent and perfumed.

Pinot Gris.desc: Alsace's richer white, weighty and smoky, dry to off-dry.

Pinot Blanc.desc: Alsace's gentle, supple everyday white and a Crémant base.

Sylvaner.desc: A crisp, understated Alsace white, lively and food-friendly.

Muscat.desc: The grapiest aromatic white — dry in Alsace, sweet in Southern vins doux naturels.

Sémillon.desc: Sauternes' golden grape, prone to noble rot; waxy and rich when dry in Graves.

Muscadelle.desc: A small aromatic component of white Bordeaux and Sauternes blends.

Marsanne.desc: The fuller-bodied white of the Northern Rhône (Hermitage Blanc), rich and nutty.

Roussanne.desc: Marsanne's finer, more aromatic Rhône partner, giving elegance and acidity.

Viognier.desc: The heady aromatic white of Condrieu in the Northern Rhône.

Clairette.desc: An old low-acid Southern white used in Rhône and Languedoc blends.

Melon de Bourgogne.desc: The grape behind Muscadet at the Loire's mouth — taut, saline, sur lie.

Aligoté.desc: Burgundy's sharp second white, bright and lemony, the classic Kir base.

Ugni Blanc.desc: A high-acid, neutral white grown widely in the South, key to Cognac/Armagnac.

Colombard.desc: A zesty South-West white (Côtes de Gascogne) full of fresh fruit.

Gros Manseng.desc: The Jurançon/Gascony white for dry, punchy, citrus-driven wines.

Petit Manseng.desc: The thick-skinned Jurançon grape for concentrated sweet and dry whites.

Petit Courbu.desc: A supple, lightly floral South-West white blended in Jurançon and Béarn.

Arrufiac.desc: A rare aromatic white of Saint-Mont and Pacherenc in the South-West.

Len de l'El.desc: Gaillac's gently aromatic white ('loin de l'œil'), soft and floral.

Mauzac.desc: The apple-scented grape of Gaillac and Limoux sparkling (Blanquette).

Savagnin.desc: The Jura's startling white grape behind oxidative Vin Jaune.

Altesse.desc: Savoie's noble white (Roussette), aromatic and ageworthy.

Gringet.desc: A rare Savoie white grown around Ayze for delicate sparkling and still wines.

Rolle.desc: The French name for Vermentino — Provence's saline, citrussy white.

Vermentinu.desc: Corsica's bright, herbal white (Vermentino), saline and fresh.

Biancu Gentile.desc: A rare indigenous Corsican white revived for its delicate aromatics.
